title: What is an ETF?
content: An ETF (Exchange-Traded Fund) is a basket of securities — like stocks or bonds — that trades on an exchange just like a single stock. ETFs typically track an index (e.g. S&P 500) and offer diversification at low cost. Unlike mutual funds, ETFs can be bought and sold throughout the trading day at market prices.

title: What is a stock?
content: A stock represents a share of ownership in a company. When you buy a stock, you become a partial owner (shareholder) and may benefit from the company's growth through price appreciation and dividends. Stocks are traded on exchanges like the NYSE or NASDAQ.

title: What is a bond?
content: A bond is a loan made by an investor to a borrower (typically a corporation or government). The borrower pays periodic interest (the coupon) and returns the principal at maturity. Bonds are generally considered lower risk than stocks but offer lower potential returns.

title: What is diversification?
content: Diversification is the practice of spreading investments across different asset classes, sectors, or geographies to reduce risk. The idea is that losses in one area may be offset by gains in another. A diversified portfolio typically includes a mix of stocks, bonds, and other assets.

title: What is dollar-cost averaging?
content: Dollar-cost averaging (DCA) is an investment strategy where you invest a fixed amount at regular intervals regardless of market conditions. This reduces the impact of volatility — you buy more shares when prices are low and fewer when prices are high, potentially lowering your average cost over time.

title: What is market capitalization?
content: Market capitalization (market cap) is the total market value of a company's outstanding shares. It is calculated as share price × total shares outstanding. Companies are often categorized as large-cap (>$10B), mid-cap ($2B–$10B), or small-cap (<$2B).

title: What is a P/E ratio?
content: The price-to-earnings (P/E) ratio compares a company's stock price to its earnings per share. A high P/E may indicate investors expect strong future growth; a low P/E may suggest the stock is undervalued or the company faces challenges. It is one of the most widely used valuation metrics.

title: What is a mutual fund?
content: A mutual fund pools money from many investors to buy a diversified portfolio of stocks, bonds, or other securities. Unlike ETFs, mutual funds are priced once per day after market close and are often actively managed by a professional fund manager. They typically carry higher fees than passive ETFs but may outperform in certain market conditions.

title: What is compound interest?
content: Compound interest is interest calculated on both the initial principal and the accumulated interest from previous periods. Often called 'interest on interest', it causes wealth to grow exponentially over time. For example, $10,000 at 8% annual return compounded over 30 years grows to approximately $100,627 — without adding a single dollar more.

title: What is inflation and how does it affect investments?
content: Inflation is the rate at which the general price level of goods and services rises, eroding purchasing power. For investors, inflation matters because it reduces the real return on investments. A 7% nominal return with 3% inflation yields only a 4% real return. Equities and real assets like real estate historically outpace inflation over the long run, while cash and fixed-rate bonds lose real value.

title: What is a dividend?
content: A dividend is a portion of a company's earnings distributed to shareholders, usually quarterly. Dividend yield is the annual dividend per share divided by the stock price. Income-focused investors often seek high-dividend stocks or dividend ETFs. Reinvesting dividends (DRIP) can significantly accelerate long-term portfolio growth through compounding.

title: What is a Roth IRA?
content: A Roth IRA is a tax-advantaged individual retirement account where contributions are made with after-tax dollars. Qualified withdrawals in retirement are completely tax-free, including all investment gains. In 2025, the contribution limit is $7,000 per year ($8,000 if age 50+). Roth IRAs are especially powerful for younger investors who expect to be in a higher tax bracket in retirement.

title: What is a 401(k)?
content: A 401(k) is an employer-sponsored retirement savings plan that allows employees to contribute pre-tax income, reducing their taxable income today. Many employers match contributions up to a certain percentage — this is essentially free money. In 2025, the employee contribution limit is $23,500. Funds grow tax-deferred until withdrawal in retirement, at which point they are taxed as ordinary income.

title: What is short selling?
content: Short selling is a strategy where an investor borrows shares and sells them, hoping to buy them back later at a lower price and pocket the difference. It is a way to profit from a declining stock. However, short selling carries theoretically unlimited risk — if the stock rises instead of falls, losses can exceed the initial investment. It is generally considered an advanced strategy.

title: What is an options contract?
content: An options contract gives the buyer the right, but not the obligation, to buy (call) or sell (put) an underlying asset at a specified price (strike price) before a set expiration date. Options can be used for hedging, income generation (covered calls), or speculation. They are leveraged instruments — small price moves in the underlying can produce large gains or losses in the option.

title: What is the difference between growth and value investing?
content: Growth investing focuses on companies expected to grow revenues and earnings faster than the market — typically tech or biotech firms with high P/E ratios. Value investing, popularized by Warren Buffett and Benjamin Graham, seeks stocks trading below their intrinsic value — often mature companies with low P/E and high dividends. Growth tends to outperform in bull markets; value tends to be more resilient in downturns.

title: What is a stock market index?
content: A stock market index tracks the performance of a group of stocks representing a segment of the market. The S&P 500 tracks 500 large US companies and is the most widely used benchmark for US equities. The Dow Jones Industrial Average (DJIA) tracks 30 blue-chip companies. The NASDAQ Composite is heavily weighted toward technology. Index funds and ETFs that track these indices offer low-cost broad market exposure.

title: What is asset allocation?
content: Asset allocation is the process of dividing a portfolio among different asset classes — stocks, bonds, cash, real estate, commodities — based on your risk tolerance, time horizon, and goals. A common rule of thumb is '100 minus your age' in stocks (e.g. 70% stocks at age 30). Rebalancing periodically restores your target allocation as markets shift. Asset allocation is widely considered the most important driver of long-term portfolio returns.

title: What is a bear market vs a bull market?
content: A bull market is a period of rising stock prices, typically defined as a 20% or more gain from a recent low. A bear market is the opposite — a 20% or more decline from a recent high. Corrections are smaller pullbacks of 10–20%. Bull markets historically last longer than bear markets. The average bull market since 1928 has lasted about 2.7 years; the average bear market about 9.6 months.

title: What is tax-loss harvesting?
content: Tax-loss harvesting is the practice of selling investments at a loss to offset capital gains taxes on profitable investments. The losses can offset gains dollar-for-dollar, and up to $3,000 of excess losses can offset ordinary income per year. The wash-sale rule prevents you from buying a substantially identical security within 30 days before or after the sale. Automated tax-loss harvesting is a key feature of many robo-advisors.
